topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box sat on my porch, an unwelcome intrusion against the peeling paint of the entryway. It was clearly addressed to the former resident, a smudge of faded handwriting marking the recipient’s name. I stared at it, my jaw tightening. I’d spent weeks meticulously cleaning this place, every surface polished to a sheen, yet this package suggested a lingering connection, an obligation I felt utterly unsuited to fulfill. A low, simmering frustration began to build in my chest. I circled the box twice, my boots scuffing the welcome mat. "This isn't my problem," I muttered to the empty air, the words tasting like ash. I picked up the box, the tape feeling brittle under my grip, and carried it straight to the curb, ready to dispose of it immediately.

[story 2]

When the delivery driver dropped the parcel off, the sound of the heavy thud echoed in the otherwise quiet apartment. I watched from the window, my hands clenching into fists at my sides. The contents, a collection of old books and faded photographs, were clearly meant for someone else. A deep furrow creased my brow. Why should I deal with this? I paced the living room floor, the rhythm of my steps sharp and uneven. Every instinct screamed at me to ignore it, to pretend the package didn't exist. I ran a hand through my hair, pulling at the strands as a tight knot formed in my throat. "It's not mine," I whispered, the conviction in my voice brittle. I decided then and there that I would leave it exactly where it lay, waiting for someone else to deal with the mess.

[story 3]

The package arrived with a dull thud, sitting squarely on the threshold. I stopped mid-task, the rag I was using to wipe down the windowsill frozen in my hand. My gaze locked onto the label, and a distinct tension radiated from my shoulders. This was an imposition, an unwelcome reminder of a past tenancy. I took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to regulate the sudden surge of irritation bubbling beneath my skin. My knuckles whitened as I examined the delivery slip. There was no sense of duty here, only a heavy sense of resistance. I walked over to the door, my movements stiff, and instead of taking it inside, I placed it on the welcome mat, a clear demarcation line between my space and whatever history this object represented.
